title: Training, parameters, and why the model has no memory

questions.question: Your company launches a new product in January 2025. An LLM with a knowledge cutoff of October 2024 is asked about it. What is the most likely outcome?
options: The model will say it doesn't know, because it was trained to admit knowledge gaps
The model will search the web automatically to find current information
The model may hallucinate a plausible-sounding but incorrect answer
The model will refuse to answer questions about products it hasn't seen
questions.explanation: The model has no knowledge of events after its training cutoff, but it doesn't 'know' it doesn't know. It will generate a statistically plausible response, which may be wrong. This is why providing current information in the prompt (RAG) matters.

questions.question: A user closes a conversation with an AI assistant and starts a new one the next day. What does the model remember from the previous conversation?
options: Everything, models retain a persistent memory of all conversations
Only the last few messages, due to context window compression
Nothing, each conversation starts fresh with no memory of previous sessions
Only things the user explicitly flagged as important
questions.explanation: LLMs have no memory between conversations. The context window is cleared when a session ends. If you want an app to remember users across sessions, you need to build that memory layer yourself, it doesn't come built-in.

questions.question: After training, a developer corrects Karel every day for a month, pointing out mistakes. How does this affect Karel's underlying parameters?
options: The parameters update gradually based on correction frequency
It has no effect, parameters are frozen after training
Karel learns from corrections but only within each session
Corrections update a separate patch layer on top of the main model
questions.explanation: After training, parameters are frozen. Chatting with Karel does not update the model. His fundamental knowledge can only change through retraining or fine-tuning, not through in-conversation corrections.

questions.question: A customer reported fraud to Karel on Monday. On Wednesday they return to follow up. Karel has no memory of the Monday conversation. What does this mean for the product team?
options: Nothing; Karel will piece together the context from the customer's new message
The team must build a memory layer that loads the prior case into Karel's context at session start
The customer must always start a new fraud report from scratch, this is expected behavior
Karel should be retrained on previous conversations to remember returning customers
questions.explanation: Memory between sessions doesn't come free, it must be deliberately designed. The product team needs to build a system that retrieves the customer's previous case and loads it into Karel's context window at the start of each new conversation.
